meta_description: In-depth analysis of Hawaii's Certificate of Need (CON) laws, examining their impact on healthcare competition, market concentration, and patient access.

section_headers.title: Scope of Regulation
section_headers.subtitle: What requires a state permission slip?

section_headers.title: Market Concentration
section_headers.subtitle: Who benefits from the lack of competition?

content_blocks: An analysis of the state's healthcare regulations and their effects on market competition and patient care.
Hawaii's score reflects a restrictive regulatory environment that stifles competition and limits consumer choice.
Hawaii's CON law applies to a broad range of healthcare facilities and services, effectively controlling market entry and expansion.
Skilled nursing facilities / Intermediate care facilities
Outpatient clinics and ambulatory care facilities
The path to approval is lengthy and provides ample opportunity for established players to block new competition.
State Health Planning and Development Agency (SHPDA)
Most communities have only one hospital system.
Despite criticism from free-market advocates, Hawaii's CON law has not been repealed and remains one of the strictest in the nation.
The state's regulatory framework creates a protectionist moat around incumbent systems.
Read our comprehensive overview of Certificate of Need laws across the United States.
Certificate of Need investigation for Washington.
$81.4B in discounted drugs. Zero disclosure requirements.
Data sourced from Hawaii Revised Statutes, SHPDA public records, and health system financial reports. Analysis by The Rojas Report.
